text: Cognitive scientist Alva Noë argues in Action in Perception that perceiving is not something that happens to us but something we do — an exercise of sensorimotor knowledge grounded in the body. When you see a tomato, you do not merely receive a two-dimensional image; you implicitly know how your movement would alter the view. This 'sensorimotor contingency' theory holds that attention is inseparable from bodily readiness. A person absorbed in a smartphone loses not only visual attention but the sensorimotor openness that keeps them oriented to surrounding space. Digital distraction is, in this sense, a narrowing of the body's perceptual grip on the environment.
question: What does Alva Noë mean by 'sensorimotor contingency' in the context of perception?

options.text: The brain's prediction of what sensory input will arrive next based on memory
Knowing how one's own movement would alter the view of a perceived object
The reflex arc that links incoming stimuli to immediate motor responses
The contingent relationship between emotional state and sensory clarity
